html:
IX

Talking of Hats

I

That was on the fifteenth afternoon of February, as I remember well.

Now those who are sensitive to any extreme condition of our climate will not have forgotten that towards the end of July of the year 1923 there was a week or ten days when the heat in London was so oppressive that frequent complaints were made at the confectioners and Soda Fountains on the ground that their ices were warm; nor were the nights less uncomfortableтБатАФтАЬuncomfortable,тАЭ that is, to quote from a gentleman who wrote to The Times about it, тАЬin a country so unprepared for any extreme of temperature that, if I do not seem too fanciful, on a cold winterтАЩs day there is nothing warm but the drinking-water and on a hot summerтАЩs day nothing cool but the sun.тАЭ Of course he did seem too fanciful, but, however that may be, the nights were certainly stifling, and one in particular I remember very well.

It was towards eleven oтАЩclock, and Hilary, Guy and I, having sat long over dinner upstairs at the Caf├й Royal, were returning towards our homes down Piccadilly, walking as slowly as we might for the prodigious heat. We had, however, barely touched the corner of Saint JamesтАЩs Street when Guy ceased even to pretend that he was walking, and said: тАЬJust a moment, will you, while I go into WhiteтАЩs to see if NapierтАЩs there, to remind him about dinner tomorrow night.тАЭ But Guy never in his life looked less like running, and Hilary said: тАЬThe idea of eating in this weather! Hm. And what is this party, Guy?тАЭ

тАЬChildrenтАЩs party,тАЭ said Guy, whose frozen blue eyes might conceivably have made one feel cool had one only been tall enough to be able to look into themтБатАКтБатАж and just at that moment, as Guy turned away, and the three of us facing down towards the Palace, Napier came swiftly down the steps of WhiteтАЩs, about ten yards down. At the curb a taxi was waiting, its door swung open.

тАЬNaps! Napier!тАЭ Guy called, thinking to catch him with as little exertion as possible in that stifling heat. But Napier, swift as a shadow, that greyhound of a Napier, was already in the taxi, the door was slammed-to, and round it swept by the Devonshire Club to turn northwards up the slope of Piccadilly.

тАЬDrat the boy!тАЭ said Guy, as we made to cross the road. тАЬCatch him on the rebound as we cross.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ But when, as the three of us stood by the island under the arch-lamp, the taxi rushed past us with screaming gears, he made no effort to hail Napier.

тАЬWell?тАЭ Hilary grinned, as the taxi tore up Albemarle Street.

тАЬOh, ring him up,тАЭ said Guy shortly, and in silence we walked towards Hyde Park Corner.

I only knew from GuyтАЩs look that he had seen her in the light that fell through the open window of the passing cab. She had seemed to be in a black dress and her head wrapped in a tight silver turban, and I had almost gasped not only with the surprise of seeing her at all, but the small face in that second of light had seemed so dazzling. тАЬNaturally,тАЭ I thought. тАЬSheтАЩs happy.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

Hilary hadnтАЩt, of course, seen her, for he was always at his most thoughtful when crossing the street. Nor had those two in the cab seen us, I was certain: they were talking too eagerly. Guy, Hilary and I walked on in silence, as slowly as we might for the heat. Maybe, I thought, Guy did not know I had seen her. As for himself, he never gave away gratuitous information about other people. And Guy loved Napier like his younger brother.

We were passing by the great gates of Devonshire House that now more becomingly adorn the Green Park when Hilary muttered тАЬBedtimeтАЭ and left us, crossing towards Half-Moon Street. I found myself walking on with Guy, despite the economy in walking I might have made by going with Hilary, for my flat also lay in that direction. But I might cut up Down Street. Guy said, as though for some minutes past he had been giving his whole mind to the matter: тАЬNot bad weather, really, if one was dressed for it.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

тАЬIf!тАЭ I said.

тАЬOf course,тАЭ said Guy, тАЬthese infernal stiff shirts.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

тАЬQuite,тАЭ I said.

тАЬAlthough,тАЭ said Guy, тАЬI think theyтАЩre cooler than those sickening soft things.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

тАЬIтАЩm wearing one,тАЭ I said.

тАЬI said what I said,тАЭ said Guy.

Once upon a time, as he had stood at the foot of her bed in a dim room, Iris had called him by a name that was not his name. тАЬBut Guy would defend a secret not only against the angels of God but also against himself.тАЭ Yes, Iris, yesтБатАКтБатАж but was it necessary, Iris, to remind him of it? For Napier was Guy de TravestтАЩs friend, and as dear to him as a younger brother.

тАЬTo swim,тАЭ Guy murmured from deep reflection, тАЬwould be very pleasant just now. Very pleasant indeed.тАЭ

тАЬYes. But where? IтАЩm not for the Loyalty, in water debauched by face-powder.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

тАЬI thought,тАЭ Guy murmured, тАЬthat I would swim at the Bath Club this afternoon. I get ideas, quick as you like. But everyone else had also been thinking on the same lines, so you can imagine the crowd. A man there told me that the best way to get in was to pick on the fattest man in the water and as he came out slip into the hole heтАЩd made. But I couldnтАЩt even see the water.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

Tall as a tree, his hat swinging lazily in his hand near his thigh, he lounged on.тБатАКтБатАж

тАЬSickening,тАЭ he murmured.

Bus after bus, laden with the people from the theatres, thundered past us and up and down the switchback, embracing us with waves of heat so that oneтАЩs very skin felt like a sticky garment.тБатАКтБатАж

тАЬYes,тАЭ I said.

тАЬLondonтАЩs all right,тАЭ said Guy thoughtfully, тАЬas London.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

тАЬOf course,тАЭ I said, тАЬas London.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

The wide sweep of Hyde Park Corner lay ahead of us like a bright handkerchief in the night. The buses trumpeted across it and around it and down it and up it, but one and all looked as snails beside Bus No.┬а16, which is beyond compare the fastest bus in London, making the voyage from Grosvenor Place to Hamilton Place and back again at a speed to astonish the eye of man.

The din that night makes in closing its doors on London was as though muted by the still, stifling air, and I envied the lofty calm of the Duke of Wellington where he rides forever amid his pleasaunce of small trees. The lights or Constitution Hill glowed like fireflies between the leafy valley of the Green Park and the dark gardens of His Majesty the King.

тАЬTrouble about London is,тАЭ said Guy thoughtfully, тАЬthat people are always expecting it to be Paris or Rome or some other place. Always wanting something else, people are.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

тАЬAnything,тАЭ I agreed, тАЬso long as itтАЩs not their own.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

тАЬThatтАЩs about it,тАЭ Guy murmured. тАЬSickening.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

We thought about that for a while.

тАЬGuy, one almost might go down to some part of the river. Near Maidenhead. Now. And swim.тАЭ

тАЬHavenтАЩt been to Maidenhead,тАЭ Guy reflected deeply, тАЬwell, it must be ten years. Difficult, isnтАЩt it, to realise itтАЩs almost ten years since that war started? I havenтАЩt beenтБатАФlet me seeтБатАФnot since the night that poor boy got himself drowned.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

тАЬOnly an hour or so by car,тАЭ I said, тАЬand you can relive your youth.тАЭ

A smile flickered across the stern, small profile. тАЬA long time to waste to relive a wasted youth. What about a game of squash instead? Makes us enjoy a drink. Come along.тАЭ

And so it came to pass that we bathed quite differently than in the river by playing squash-racquets by electric-light. Guy has a court in the basement of his house, and when he beats you, which is always, he says: тАЬSickening.тАЭ

тАЬWhere,тАЭ I asked, when we had bathed sufficiently and were enjoying long tumblers of the stuff that such good jokes are made from, whilst from upstairs came the faint notes of a piano and a thing they call a saxophone, for Lady de Travest was тАЬthrowingтАЭ a small party; тАЬwhere are we dining tomorrow night? And, now I come to think of it, why this sudden childrenтАЩs party?тАЭ

Guy had happened on Venice playing tennis the other day, when she had said she was feeling perhaps a little depressed. тАЬThe heat,тАЭ she had said.тБатАКтБатАж

тАЬWhereupon,тАЭ said Guy, imitating Cherry-Marvel, тАЬit came to me as not a bad idea if we had a party for the child. Real good girl, Venice. Hope that young man of mine will find someone only half so good.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

тАЬYes,тАЭ I said.

тАЬBe a sort of family party, I thought. Hugo and Shirley, Napier and Venice, some clean and wholesome young woman IтАЩll find for you, while I, thank the Lord, will be odd man out. But as to where we should dine.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

тАЬIn this heat.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

тАЬGod, yes, too hot for dancing. Just listen to them upstairs! Even the ceilingтАЩs sweating.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

The faint, slow lilt of the tango, pleasantest of all dances but one that is so seldom danced in London because nobody in London can dance it, which seems a pity.тБатАКтБатАж

тАЬMight almost dine here,тАЭ Guy murmured, тАЬif Moira doesnтАЩt want the place. And we might, now youтАЩve suggested it, and if itтАЩs still so hot, go and bathe somewhere afterwards instead of sitting up in some stuffy place till all hours. See how we feel about it, and if Venice would enjoy that.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

тАЬImagine Venice not enjoying that!тАЭ

тАЬWell, weтАЩll see,тАЭ said Guy, but more seriously now. тАЬIf we do, it will mean no cocktails before dinner, no more than a glass of wine apiece over dinner, and not a thimbleful after. IтАЩm not going to have that river play any more tricks on my friends, I can tell you.тАЭ

тАЬAnd decency, Guy, will be more than served, for thereтАЩs no moon and the nights are pitch-black.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

тАЬThatтАЩs right,тАЭ said Guy thoughtfully, and then, as he saw me to the door, he said thoughtfully: тАЬBy the way, you any idea if Venice has ever met Iris?тАЭ

тАЬI donтАЩt think so,тАЭ I said. тАЬBut IтАЩm not sure.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

There is never any harm in saying one isnтАЩt sure. One should never be sure, conversationally.

тАЬI just had an idea,тАЭ Guy murmured, looking out over the heavy trees of the great square, тАЬthat Iris might conceivably be passing through London, as I heard from Eve Chalice today that old Portairley was lying near death. The last Portairley, dear, dear.тАЭ

тАЬGerald wonтАЩt be sorry to have missed his turn, IтАЩve no doubt.тАЭ

тАЬPoor young devil! But what I was thinking of was, just in case Iris is in London, that we might get her for the third woman tomorrow night.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

тАЬOh,тАЭ I said. тАЬI see.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

тАЬYouтАЩd quite like that, wouldnтАЩt you?тАЭ

тАЬOh, IтАЩd like it!тАЭ

тАЬJust had an idea,тАЭ Guy murmured vaguely, тАЬthat she and Venice might meet, if they havenтАЩt already met, and see how they like each other. That is, if Iris is in London. Different typesтБатАКтБатАж you never know. Tell Iris, if by any chance you hear anything of her tomorrow. My idea, tell her.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

тАЬAll right, if she should give me a ring. Good night, Guy.тАЭ

тАЬGood night, boy. Sorry about the squash. Sickening. My idea, tell her.тАЭ

As I looked back from that wide corner of Belgrave Square which sweeps suavely up to Hyde Park Corner, I could see the very tall figure of the friend of his friends still framed against the lighted doorway. Across the four open windows above him figures passed slowly.

But what, what in the world, could suddenly have happened to Iris, she whom I had last seen, whom I had last heard, saying she would nevermore return to England, promisingтБатАКтБатАжтАК? And one realised, in wondering that with so deep a bewilderment, how very literally one would take IrisтАЩs word, how completely one had believed in her promise, as one would have believed in any promise made by that Iris March who, as Hilary had reluctantly to confess, did not lie. But nowтБатАКтБатАж nevermore, nevermore!

And as I let myself into my flat, I found myself picturing Guy de Travest and Iris face to face in a place where no people were, Guy and Iris completely alone with each other and God. And it was Guy whom I heard speaking, GuyтАЩs low cold voice telling Iris of certain things, how he had been shocked that dim morning to hear her whisper a name like a kiss, a name that was already pledged to another, and how, when he had long since forgotten her whispering of that name, he had chanced on a night to see her no further than the span of that name apart from him who bore it, and how he couldnтАЩt but think that she was committing the one unpardonable crime of stealing a man from his wife, like a mean little thief in the night. And I could imagine Iris in her tight silver turban, like a star it would be in that lonely place where she faced Guy, and her tiger-tawny curls dancing formally on each small check, and all about her that dazzling brilliance which will suddenly enwrap a very fair woman in a black dress, whilst the blood would be clean emptied from her small grave face as she listened to the judgment of the slender giant with the cold eyes and the quiet, so quiet, savage voice. They were of the same people, Guy and Iris, of the same blood, of the same landscape, and you couldnтАЩt help but wonder how she would face his judgment, she who had for so long outlawed herself, she who so profoundly impressed you as not caring the tremor of an eyelash for the laws of her fathers. Would she, faced by the warrior of conduct, still not care, or would she be ashamed and afraid, would she be as though seeing England, her England, the very soil of her England, turning from her in contempt? I simply could not tell what she would feel, so little did I know of the nature of that shameless, shameful lady. And that was again the thought that came to me the very next night to the one I am telling of, whilst I sat beside her in her car, and we in the van of the childrenтАЩs partyтАЩs raid on the river. A torment of heat lay over England that July night, but that is not why we who sped through the countryside will remember it.

She was driving, and when I dropped a word into the silence of our drive, for Iris and I were at enmity nowтБатАФfor Venice!тБатАФa curious smile seemed to devour the white profile, to devour it quite: a very witch of a smile that was, I thought, and more than adequate to meet my word, for the word I had dropped was what the raven quoth: тАЬNevermore!тАЭ

But as she smiled so, she drove that menacing bonnet ever more furiously along the road to Maidenhead, so that corners perished like midgets before our headlights and Hugo and Shirley, who sat behind, murmured against her driving, saying that it would be bad for their reputation as a happily-married couple to be found dead on the road to Maidenhead. тАЬA friend of mine,тАЭ yelled Hugo, тАЬwas asked to resign from BuckтАЩs for being found dead on the Maidenhead road.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

But Iris drove faster and ever faster, and suddenly I realised that the rare devouring smile that was like my enemy on her face was new to me who had never before seen Iris smile happily.

II

I have gone too far ahead in the tale of the last March, letting myself be beguiled from a narratorтАЩs duties by the reckless flight of the silver stork through the quiet countryside. But from the night of the childrenтАЩs party I can only go back by saying that she was wearing that night not her silver turban but a green hat, yea, a green hat, of a sort of felt, and bravely worn; and who but I had bought that green hat for her that very day, she having said to me after luncheon that she needed a green hat pour le sport. I understood that the sport would be under even warmer skies than ours, for in three daysтАЩ time, she said, she would be on board ship for Rio di Janeiro, and she did not need to tell me that she would not be voyaging unaccompanied. That was a fell lady for whom I bought a green hat that day.

Nothing easier than a green hat, it appears, can well be bought. Like a flash of summer lightning, that is how a green hat is bought. Says the lady to the shop: тАЬGreeting, sir. I will have a green hat pour le sport, similar in every way to the green hats I have bought here every year since the death of Dr.┬аCrippen.тАЭ

тАЬVery good, madam. That will be so much, madam. On your account, madam?тАЭ

тАЬOh, no! My friend will pay. Farewell.тАЭ

We spoke very little over the luncheon we took together. It was a stifling day, and what, anyhow, was there to say? Very far from my business was it to speak of broken promises unless spoken to, and very far from her thoughts did any question of broken promises seem. Oh, but that was a fell lady who luncheoned with me on that sweltering day!

We sat picking at green olives and salads and bits of toast, we drank those long iced drinks full of vegetable matter which, apparently, one must drink so that one may feel the heat more poignantly than before, we had nothing in particular to say. Early that morning she had rung me up, a calm, happy voice, demanding from me not the smallest expression of surprise at her presence in London; although, of course, one did make a show of being surprised, for she couldnтАЩt possibly know that I had seen her in that cab, and, I thought, she never would know. The Marches would be let off that, anyhow.

But Iris, over that luncheon, did not appear to remark that I had nothing in particular to say. And, what with the heat and with that, I suppose I grew more and more annoyed, for there isnтАЩt, I suppose, anything in the world more irritating than to be angry with a woman and she not notice it at all. Of course many women will appear not to notice it, but you can see that that is put on; but this Iris just, IтАЩll swear it, did not notice anything.

Nor, I thought, did she have a very healthy appetite for one not long since recovered from a serious illness, the way she picked at bits of things here and there; but she excused herself to Charles, who came up to protest against the dishonour she did his food, on the ground that she never did eat with her meals.

And then there was a moment when I asked, from a large silence which seemed to her maddeningly natural, I just asked paternally, since it is always easier to be paternal than to be fraternal: тАЬHappy, Iris?тАЭ

She was buttering a piece of toast Melba about half an inch square. My question stayed her knife. She stared intently towards the doors of the restaurant for a long second, and then she said, frankly, gravely, calmly, not at all intensely but with unutterable conviction: тАЬUnbearably.тАЭ Then she went on buttering her piece of toast Melba, and I could do what I liked about it.

Now I must say this for the Iris who sat with her profile to me that day, that she was a more lovely Iris even than the one I had known. But as to how she was more lovely, that I do not know; nor, if I knew, could I describe it but by using the word тАЬethereal,тАЭ to be immediately followed by the word тАЬunearthly,тАЭ for it is a convention not to be broken lightly that a woman who has not long since recovered from a long illness must look тАЬetherealтАЭ and тАЬunearthly.тАЭ But she didnтАЩt, I think, look either of those two things. She seemed, I mean to say, more lovely than ever just because she was more earthy. She looked, I fancy I mean, in loveтБатАФher skin, that is to say, looked as though she who wore it was in love. Yes, her skin did. I fancy it must have been that. A beautiful woman in love and loved seems, in however unaware a moment, to glow with an earthy beauty. When writers say that тАЬGloria was looking very spiritual that morningтАЭ what they really meanтБатАФof course, this is all theoryтБатАФis that Gloria was looking more earthy that morning, that in her eyes there was the afterglow of loveтАЩs delight. A beautiful woman neglected or unloved appeals, of course, more to the chivalrous sense in men, for men will stand more of a chance of a sad woman being interested in them; but the very skin of a woman who is coiled in love seems to have a jewel-like quality, and her mind is like a temptation one wants to touch.

тАЬAnd,тАЭ I said, fascinated for some reason by the faint, faint golden down on her arm, тАЬyouтАЩre quite well and strong now?тАЭ

тАЬOf course,тАЭ she said, тАЬnot as strong as all that. But strong enough.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

тАЬOh, dear! Strong enough for what, Iris?тАЭ

тАЬEverything,тАЭ she said, shrouding a boiled cherry in whipped cream. тАЬMust get fat,тАЭ she explained as an afterthought.

Now there were two red camellias painted on the left side of the crown of her hatтБатАФwomen at that time didnтАЩt wear bowler hats, or, as they prefer to call them, cloche hatsтБатАФwhich was of the same colour as the sun, of straw, and with a narrow stiff brim. The two red camellias looked just as waxen and artificial as two real red camellias would look, and so it must have cost a power of money, that hat. She would have flown like the wind to Reboux in Paris, saying to herself: тАЬI am in love. I must have a hat,тАЭ and so she had bought that hat. As for her dress that stifling day, you would have called it blue if you hadnтАЩt seen that no colour made by hands could compare with the blue of those grave eyes, and it was of that fine texture which is finer than the texture of silk of China, if such a thing can be, and here and there upon its lower parts were worked large white arabesques in what looked to an uninformed eye like wool, but surely it could not be the fleece of the lamb that Iris was wearing that day?

тАЬAnd did Guy,тАЭ she asked, тАЬsay anything when you three saw me in that cab last night?тАЭ

тАЬOh!тАЭ I said.

She had very suddenly turned to me, so that at last I must look full into the eyes that blazed so incredibly blue from the shadow of the yellow hatтБатАКтБатАж and I, I could not meet those eyes! I stared instead at the emerald on the third finger of her right hand, and how white and frail that hand looked, so weak, so frail, when you thought of it as belonging to those deep, compelling, unscrupulous eyes.

тАЬWell?тАЭ It was her voice, faint, slightly husky; yet it rose above the roar of London and was lost in the clouds that pass over a strange, unknown land.

тАЬPersonally,тАЭ I said, тАЬI liked your silver turban very much.тАЭ

тАЬDear, that was not a turban!тАЭ

тАЬTurban is a pretty word, Iris. And suitable, too.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

тАЬTurkey, polygamy?тАЭ

тАЬJust a boyish fancy.тАЭ

тАЬAnd Guy? You havenтАЩt told me?тАЭ

тАЬBut, Iris, he never, as you know, gives away gratuitous information. He just asked me to ask you to dine tonight, as I have done. тАШMy idea, tell her,тАЩ he said. In fact, he repeated that. And youтАЩre coming?тАЭ

тАЬWhy, of course!тАЭ she said absently, so absently.

тАЬBut why do you ask about Guy, Iris? I fancied you didnтАЩt care what anyone thought.тАЭ

Throughout that passage her face had been turned to mine, but only now could I master the courage to raise my eyes from the third finger of her right hand, to see that her face was as though turned to a mask of white stone with two amethysts for eyes. It was a mask, that face, and those were the eyes of a mask. Yet it was far from a mask of concealment, it was the mask of herself, of her very self, of the self that was, in some remote part of her being, really herself. And again I couldnтАЩt help thinking of her as of someone who had strayed into our world from a strange land unknown to us, a land where lived a race of men and women who were calmly awaiting their inheritance of our world when we should have annihilated one another in our endless squabbles about honour, morality, nationality. Strong were the people of that land, stronger than the gold they despised but used, deterred by not qualm nor fear, strong and undefeatable. And just like that was the white mask of this beautiful woman, strong and undefeatable. It knew not truth nor lying, not honour nor dishonour, not loyalty nor treachery, not good nor evil: it was profoundly itself, a mask of the morning of this world when men needed not to confuse their minds with laws with which to confuse their neighbours, a mask of the evening of this world when men shall have at last made passions their servants and can enter into their full inheritance.тБатАКтБатАж

тАЬI donтАЩt,тАЭ she said at last from a remote distance, the amethysts absorbed in the air between us. тАЬI donтАЩt.тАЭ And then she smiled faintly, but even so much was enough to change the amethysts into eyes. тАЬI donтАЩt,тАЭ she said very huskily. тАЬBut I just asked.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

тАЬIris,тАЭ I said, my mind charged with that mask, тАЬyou have us all at a great disadvantage.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

Slowly, thoughtfully, she made a circle of air with a small golden tube that had a crimson tongue, and then she passed the golden tube through the circleтАЩs heart. She was thinking.
